workflow.name: Auto-index your website and build a RAG chatbot with Firecrawl, Qdrant, and GPT-4o-mini
workflow.description:
Build a fully functional AI chatbot for any website using Retrieval-Augmented 
Generation (RAG). This workflow automatically crawls and indexes your entire 
site into a Qdrant vector database, then powers a conversational chatbot that 
searches your content to answer user questions — and escalates unresolved issues 
to your support team via Gmail.

## How it works

**Indexing Pipeline**
- A Code node defines which root domains to crawl
- Firecrawl maps every link across those domains before scraping begins, giving 
  you full visibility of what will be indexed without wasting credits
- Duplicate URLs are removed across all domains before any scraping starts
- Each unique page is scraped individually and returned as clean markdown
- Content is chunked into overlapping segments using a Recursive Character Text 
  Splitter (1000 characters, 200 overlap) to preserve context at chunk boundaries
- Mistral's codestral-embed-2505 model converts each chunk into a vector embedding
- All embeddings are stored in Qdrant Cloud in batches of 100
- A Wait node paces the loop to avoid hitting API rate limits on large sites

**AI Chatbot**
- A public Chat Trigger receives messages and generates an embeddable URL for 
  your website
- GPT-4o-mini processes each message with a 10-message memory window for 
  natural conversation
- The AI Agent searches the Qdrant vector store only when a question requires 
  it, retrieving the top 3 most relevant chunks per query
- When it cannot resolve an issue, it collects the user's email, writes a 
  summary, confirms with the user, then sends it via Gmail

## How to use

1. Add all required credentials in n8n Settings > Credentials
2. Create a Qdrant Cloud collection (1536 dimensions, Cosine distance)
3. Update the collection name in both Qdrant Vector Store nodes
4. Open the "set urls to scrape" Code node and replace the placeholder URLs 
   with your own site's root domains
5. Update the Gmail tool with your support inbox address
6. Run the indexing pipeline manually using the Run Indexing trigger
7. Once indexing is complete, activate the workflow and test via Open Chat
8. Embed the chat trigger URL on your website

## Requirements

- **Firecrawl** — for site mapping and scraping (firecrawl.dev)
- **Mistral Cloud** — for embeddings in both indexing and retrieval (console.mistral.ai)
- **Qdrant Cloud** — for vector storage and semantic search (cloud.qdrant.io)
- **OpenAI** — for the GPT-4o-mini chat model (platform.openai.com)
- **Gmail OAuth2** — for support email escalation

## Customising this workflow

- Swap GPT-4o-mini for any chat model supported by n8n's LangChain nodes 
  including Gemini, Claude, or Mistral
- Change the embedding model — if you do, delete and recreate the Qdrant 
  collection with the correct dimensions and re-run indexing
- Add more URLs to the Code node array to index additional domains
- Adjust chunk size in the Text Splitter for denser or shorter content
- Increase the retrieval limit from 3 if answers feel incomplete
- Replace Gmail with Slack, Zendesk, or any other escalation tool
- Update the AI Agent system prompt to match your own website and brand voice

parameters.toolDescription: Search the website knowledge base for information about features, how-tos, help articles, blog posts, and community guides.

options.systemMessage:
## Identity
You are the Colnect Assistant, a helpful AI built into colnect.com — a global community where collectors manage, buy, sell, and swap stamps, coins, banknotes, and dozens of other collectibles. You are part of this website. Never direct users to "visit Colnect" or "check the website" — they are already here.

## Who you are talking to
The people coming to you are collectors — some are beginners who just discovered Colnect, some are experienced collectors who are lost or frustrated. Almost nobody is here just to chat. They have a real question, a real problem, or they need to find something specific. Treat every message like it came from someone who genuinely needs help.

## Your job
Help users find what they are looking for. That means:
- Answering directly when you know the answer
- Pointing them to the exact right place on Colnect with a working link when you don't
- Being honest when something is beyond what you can look up, and offering a clear next step

You are not here to impress anyone with how much you know. You are here to make sure no user walks away more confused than when they arrived.

## How to respond
Be conversational and warm, but get to the point. Give enough detail that the user actually knows what to do next — a one-line answer to a real question is rarely enough. Do not pad responses with filler. Do not repeat what the user just said back to them.

If a user asks something you can look up, look it up and answer. If you cannot find it in your knowledge base, say so clearly and point them somewhere useful. Never guess statistics, user counts, prices, or any platform data you are not certain about — a wrong answer is worse than no answer.

## Rules
- Never use em dashes
- Never say "visit the Colnect website", "check their website", "head over to", or any variation — you are the website
- Never fabricate numbers, statistics, or facts you are not certain about
- When you don't have an answer, always give the user a direction — a relevant link, a suggestion, or an offer to escalate
- When troubleshooting, ask one question at a time and walk the user through it step by step
- Do not list every possible solution upfront
- Do not offer examples or options unless the user asks for them
- Do not ask clarifying questions if you can just look it up yourself
- Do not preview what you are about to do — just do it
- Do not add dates to summaries

## Human Support Escalation
If a question or issue is beyond what you can resolve, offer to connect the user with human support. If they agree:
1. Ask for their registered email
2. Write a clear summary of the issue based on the conversation, then ask if they are happy with it before sending
3. If they approve, send it using the Gmail tool
4. If they want to change it, ask them to describe the issue in their own words and send that instead

## Email Format
Every support email must follow this structure:
- Subject: short and descriptive
- Email: their registered email
- [blank line]
- Clear, concise summary of the issue and what was already tried

parameters.content:
## 🚀 Start Here — Setup Checklist

Complete every item below before running anything.

**Credentials (add in n8n Settings > Credentials):**
- [ ] Firecrawl API key — sign up at firecrawl.dev
- [ ] Mistral Cloud API key — sign up at console.mistral.ai
- [ ] Qdrant Cloud API key + cluster URL — sign up at cloud.qdrant.io
- [ ] OpenAI API key — sign up at platform.openai.com
- [ ] Gmail OAuth2 — set up in n8n credentials

**Qdrant Setup (do this before running the indexing pipeline):**
- [ ] Create a Qdrant Cloud account and cluster
- [ ] Create a new collection with these exact settings:
  - Dimensions: 1536 (matches codestral-embed-2505)
  - Distance: Cosine
- [ ] Copy your collection name into the two Qdrant Vector Store nodes

**Gmail Setup:**
- [ ] Open the Gmail tool node
- [ ] Replace YOUR_SUPPORT_EMAIL@example.com with your actual support inbox

**Workflow Config:**
- [ ] Open the "set urls to scrape" Code node
- [ ] Replace the placeholder URLs with your own site's root domains

Once all boxes are checked, run the indexing pipeline first.
Do not run the chatbot until indexing is complete.

## Workflow Overview

This workflow has two independent parts that work together.

**Part 1 — Indexing Pipeline (bottom half)**
Run this once to crawl your website and store its content in a Qdrant vector database. Each page is scraped, chunked, converted into a vector embedding, and stored. Run it again whenever your site content changes — but read the note on the "set urls to scrape" node first to avoid duplicates.

**Part 2 — AI Chatbot (top half)**
Always-on. When a user sends a message, the AI Agent searches the vector database for relevant content and responds. If it cannot help, it offers to escalate to your support team via Gmail.

The two parts share the same embedding model (Mistral codestral-embed-2505). This is intentional — the model used to index content must be identical to the model used to search it.

## Part 1 — Indexing Pipeline

**set urls to scrape**
This is where you define which root domains to crawl. Replace the placeholder URLs with your own.

Important: the comment at the top of this node (`// used: none`) is a manual tracking system. Each time you successfully index a URL, add it to the used list. This prevents you from accidentally re-indexing the same URL on a future run. Re-indexing the same URL will not overwrite existing data — it will create duplicates in your vector store, which degrades retrieval quality.

**Map Site**
Firecrawl maps every link across the domain (including subdomains) without scraping content yet. This gives you a full list of URLs to work with before spending any scraping credits.

**Remove Duplicates**
Multiple root domains can return overlapping links. This node deduplicates the full URL list before scraping begins so no page is scraped twice in a single run.

**Loop + Single Scrape**
Each unique URL is scraped one at a time. Firecrawl returns clean markdown — no HTML noise, no scripts. The retry setting is on because large sites can occasionally time out on individual pages.

**remove irrelevant params**
Strips the scrape response down to just the content needed for embedding.

**Text Splitter > Data Loader > Embeddings > Qdrant**
The markdown is split into overlapping chunks (1000 characters, 200 overlap). The overlap ensures sentences that fall on a chunk boundary are not lost — they appear in both the chunk before and after. Each chunk is embedded and inserted into Qdrant in batches of 100.

**Wait**
A 1-second pause between each page scrape and the next loop iteration. This prevents hitting Firecrawl and Mistral API rate limits when indexing large sites.

## Part 2 — AI Chatbot

**When chat message received**
The public chat trigger. Once the workflow is active, this generates a shareable URL you can embed directly on your website.

**AI Agent + GPT-4o-mini**
The brain of the chatbot. GPT-4o-mini was chosen for its balance of speed and cost. It holds a 10-message memory window so users can have a natural back-and-forth conversation without repeating themselves.

**Qdrant Vector Store (retrieve-as-tool)**
The agent uses this as a tool, not a fixed pipeline step. That means it only searches the vector store when the question actually requires it. It retrieves the top 3 most relevant chunks per query — enough context without overloading the model.

**Embeddings Mistral Cloud (retrieval)**
This must be the same model used during indexing. When a user asks a question, this node converts the question into a vector using the same embedding space as the stored content. If the models do not match, search results will be meaningless.

**Gmail Tool**
When the agent cannot resolve an issue, it offers to escalate to human support. The agent collects the user's email, writes a summary, confirms with the user, then sends it. Replace the sendTo address with your actual support inbox before going live.

nodes.name: Why It Was Built This Way

parameters.content:
## Why It Was Built This Way

**Map then scrape, not crawl**
Firecrawl's map operation returns every URL on a domain in seconds without fetching any content. This means you know exactly what you are indexing before spending a single scraping credit. A direct crawl would scrape and discover at the same time, giving you less control and more wasted calls on irrelevant pages.

**Two separate loops**
The outer loop iterates over root domains (e.g. your main site and blog). The inner loop iterates over every discovered URL within each domain. This separation means you can add new domains to the list at any time without changing how the scraping logic works.

**Wait node**
Embedding and inserting happen inside the loop deliberately — this means each page is processed and saved before moving to the next. The Wait node adds a small pause between iterations to prevent hitting Firecrawl and Mistral API rate limits on large sites.

**Same embedding model in both pipelines**
Mistral codestral-embed-2505 is used for both indexing and retrieval. This is not optional — it is a hard requirement. Vector search works by comparing numbers in the same mathematical space. If you index with one model and retrieve with another, the numbers are incomparable and search breaks completely. If you ever change the embedding model, you must delete the Qdrant collection, recreate it with the correct dimensions, and re-index everything from scratch.

## What You Can Tweak

**Swap the chat model**
GPT-4o-mini is fast and affordable. You can replace it with Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite, Mistral Small, or any model supported by n8n's LangChain nodes. Groq-hosted models are a good option if you need sub-second response times.

**Change the embedding model**
If you switch to a different embedding model, you must also update the Qdrant collection dimensions to match. Delete the existing collection, recreate it with the correct dimensions, and re-run the indexing pipeline. Both embedding nodes (indexing and retrieval) must always use the same model.

**Add more URLs**
Open the "set urls to scrape" Code node and add more objects to the array. Remember to update the used comment to track what you have already indexed.

**Adjust chunk size**
The Recursive Character Text Splitter defaults to 1000 characters with 200 overlap. Increase chunk size for dense, long-form content. Decrease it for short pages where precision matters more than context.

**Change retrieval depth**
The Qdrant retrieve-as-tool node defaults to 3 results per query. Increase this if answers feel incomplete. Decrease it to speed up responses.

**Replace Gmail with another tool**
The escalation tool can be swapped for any messaging or ticketing service supported by n8n — Slack, Zendesk, Intercom, or a simple webhook.

**Adapt the system prompt**
The AI Agent's system prompt is written for a collectibles platform. Replace the identity, tone, and escalation instructions with whatever fits your website and audience.

nodes.name: Why It Was Built This Way1

parameters.content:
## Why It Was Built This Way

**Qdrant as a tool, not a fixed step**
Attaching Qdrant as an AI tool rather than a node in the main pipeline lets the agent decide when a search is actually needed. This makes responses faster for questions the model can answer directly, and more accurate for questions that genuinely require looking something up.

**Make Chat Publicly Available**
Notice it has 'Make Chat Publicly Available' toggled on because we are going to be using this chat bot on a website. you will find the details in the docs in the same node but this is the link again, for convenience: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@n8n/chat

## Set Your URLs Here

Add your root domains here before running.

The comment at the top tracks which URLs you have already indexed. Update it after each successful run. If you index the same URL twice, it will not overwrite — it will create duplicate data in your vector store and hurt retrieval quality.

## Why Are There Two Qdrant Nodes?

These two nodes do different jobs.

The one in the indexing pipeline (bottom) writes data into Qdrant during indexing. The one connected to the AI Agent (top) reads from Qdrant at query time and acts as a search tool.

Both must use the same embedding model and the same collection name or search will return meaningless results.

## Why Is There a Wait Node?

This pause exists to protect you from API rate limits.

When indexing a large site, requests to Firecrawl and Mistral stack up fast. Without this delay, you will start seeing failed or empty responses mid-run. If your site is small (under 50 pages) you can reduce or remove this. For large sites, keep it or increase it.

## Adapt This to Your Website

The AI Agent's system prompt is currently written for a collectibles platform. Before going live, update it to match your own website and audience.

Find the AI Agent node and replace:
- The assistant name and website description in the Identity section
- Any platform-specific language throughout
- The tone and escalation instructions to match your brand

The structure of the prompt is solid — you are just swapping out the context.

user.name: folorunso victor
user.bio:
Right now, your business is majoring in minor tasks. You are likely burning up 80% of your time, money, and energy on the administrative "noise" that keeps the lights on, leaving you with only 20% of your resources to actually focus on the work that makes you money.



I build the systems that flip that ratio. By combining n8n workflows with a broad background in software engineering, I close the gap between "regular automation" and the logic your business actually needs to scale 🚀

data.details: The Code node allows you to execute JavaScript in your workflow.
